segments.s145-g1: Subject (گرازان) precedes a motion phrase; به درگاه شاه is a direction/prepositional phrase meaning 'into the king's court/prescence' (به + درگاه + اسمِ مالکِ شاه). آمدند is the simple past plural 'they came.' The clause is straightforward S (subject) + prepositional complement + verb typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s145-g2: Two adjectival/participial descriptors (گشاده‌دل and نیک‌خواه) linked by و describe the manner/character of the subject; آمدند repeats the verb for emphasis (stylistic repetition common in poetry), so the verb again applies to those descriptors: 'they came open‑hearted and well‑wishing.'
segments.s145-g3: چو here = 'when.' Two coordinated past verbs (رفتند 'they went' and بردند 'they carried/brought') describe sequential actions; پیشش is the preposition پیش 'before/in the presence of' with the enclitic -ش 'him' (object/locative reference). نماز is the noun object ('prayer') placed after the prepositional phrase — a poetic but clear time/place/verb ordering.
segments.s145-g4: برآشفت is a past tense (he/it became angry; prefix بر- + آشفَت) with its subject understood from context. Then و links to پاسخ نداد: پاسخ 'answer' + نداد 'did not give' (داد in past, here negative by context—see ایچ). ایچ باز is an emphatic classical negator meaning 'not at all'/'nothing whatsoever,' intensifying that no reply was given.
segments.s145-g5: یکی functions as an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one.' پانگ (بانگ) is 'a cry/crying out' and برزد is an archaic/past-form verb (بر- + زد) meaning 'uttered/let fall (a cry),' followed by the prepositional phrase به گیو 'to Giv.' از نخست is a temporal phrase 'from the first' = 'at first.' Note the poetic, slightly inverted ordering.
segments.s145-g6: پس آنگاه is a doubled temporal marker 'then/afterwards' for emphasis. شرم is the object 'shame' and از دو دیده 'from the two eyes' gives the locus from which it was washed; بشست is past 'he washed.' The subject is implicit (contextually Giv), so the clause reads 'then he washed the shame from his two eyes.'
segments.s145-g7: The repeated که introduces a rhetorical/relative clause: 'که رستم که باشد' = 'who is Rostam that...' باشد here has a subjunctive/optative flavor ('should be'/'would be') in rhetorical questioning. فرمان من ('my command') is the object/topic that the subsequent verbs will act upon. Multiple كه are a stylistic device to build the rhetorical question.
segments.s145-g8: کند and پیچد are present/subjunctive-style verbs used to express the hypothetical or potential actions ('would do / would turn'). کند پست literally 'make (it) low' = 'treat [my command] as worthless' and پیچد ز پیمان من 'twist/turn from my covenant' = 'break/turn away from my oath.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and من is the possessor 'my.'
segments.s145-g17: خود از جای برخاست is a compact clause meaning “he himself rose from his place”: خود is emphatic (himself), از جای is a prepositional phrase (from his place) and برخاست is the simple past (3rd singular) of برخاستن “to rise/get up.” کاووس کی begins a relative clause: کاووس is the proper name and کی here functions as a relative pronoun “who” linking to what follows.
segments.s145-g18: برافروخت is past tense (3rd sg.) “kindled/lit.” برسان here functions as ‘to send/bring’ (a purposive infinitive/verb of sending in this poetic context) — the clause says he lit a fire for sending; آتش ز نی is the object phrase “a fire/from a reed” with ز (poetic form of از) marking source and نی meaning ‘reed’ (a reed used as a torch or signaler).
segments.s145-g19: بشد here is an archaic past from شدن meaning roughly “rose/it happened” (3rd sg.) and is the verb governing طوس (Tūs) as subject. The sequence دست تهمتن گرفت is SOV: دست (hand) + تهمتن (Tahmatan, name/epithet) as possessive/object without an explicit ezāfe (poetic omission), then گرفت (past 3rd sg. ‘seized/took’).
segments.s145-g20: بدو is the dative/adverbial ‘to him/unto him.’ مانده is a past participle (or stative past) “remained/was left,” with the copula omitted (typical in classical Persian). پرخاش جویان is a compound: پرخاش ‘reproach/attack’ + جویان ‘-seekers’ = ‘those seeking quarrel/attackers.’ شگفت is a predicate adjective meaning ‘astonishing/astonished’ — here describing his state.
segments.s145-g21: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “who/that.” از پیش here means “from before / in front of / in the presence of.” کاووس is the proper name (Kavus). بیرون برد is a compound verbal phrase: بیرون (out/away) + برد (past of بردن ‘to take/bring’) = “led/took out” (3rd sg.). The clause therefore says someone was taken out in front of Kavus.
segments.s145-g22: مگر is an excluding conjunction here (“except that”). کاندر is an archaic fused preposition (کَـ + اندر) meaning “in that / in which.” آن points back to ‘that (moment/condition).’ تیزی is used as ‘swiftness/quickness’ (not only literal sharpness). افسون is the noun “spell/enchantment,” and برد (past of بردن/برد) here means ‘prevailed/took effect’ — i.e. the charm carried the day.
segments.s145-g23: تهمتن is the subject (the epithet/name). برآشفت is past tense (3rd sg.) meaning “became angry/grew furious.” با governs the object in the sense “with” and شهریار is the noun ‘king’ (the target of his anger): literally “Tahmatan grew angry with the king.”
segments.s145-g24: که introduces the explanatory clause “for/that.” چندین is a quantifier “several/many a.” مدار آتش is a noun phrase “circles/rings of fire” (مدار = circuit/circle, آتش = fire). اندر کنار is a locative phrase “at the side/alongside” (اندر = in/within; کنار = beside), so the clause says that several rings/circuits of fire were beside (him/there).
segments.s145-g161: همی is an archaic/poetic continuative particle here meaning "kept on" or "continuously." باده خوردند is a past-tense verb phrase (خوردند, 3rd person plural past) literally 'they ate/consumed wine' — in Persian خوردن with باده means 'to drink.' تا نیم شب is a temporal complement 'until midnight' (نیم شب = 'mid-night' / 'middle of the night').
segments.s145-g162: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/at/by' used here to mark accompaniment or source ('to/with the minstrels'). خنیاگران is the plural 'minstrels/singers.' برگشاده is a prefixed/adjectival form meaning 'open' or 'having opened' and it describes دو لب ('two lips' = 'their mouths'); the phrase thus means 'the minstrels with open mouths' (i.e., singing). Word order and the compound adjectival form are typical of classical Persian poetic diction.
segments.s145-g25: همه کارت is a noun phrase meaning 'all your actions/deeds' (همه preposes کارت). از یکدگر introduces the comparative clause 'than one another' (از = 'than/from' in comparisons), and بدترست is the predicative adjective 'is worse' with the old copular ending -ست (‘is’). Word order is subject/phrase + comparative phrase + predicate.
segments.s145-g26: ترا here is the oblique form 'to/for you' (an enclitic oblique pronoun written as ت + را). شهریاری is a noun 'kingship/sovereignty', and نه اندرخورست is a negated predicate: نه (not) + اندرخور (fitting/worthy) + -ست (is). The sentence reads literally 'Kingship is not fitting for you.'
segments.s145-g27: تو is the emphatic/subject 'you' addressing the addressee; سهراب را marks Sohrab as the definite object with را. زنده modifies the object ('alive'), and بر دار کن is an imperative: بر + دار ('onto the gallows') + کن (imperative 'do/make') — literally 'put (him) alive on the gallows.' The verb کن at the end gives the command.
segments.s145-g28: پرآشوب و بدخواه را is a coordinated object phrase 'the turbulent and the ill-wisher' (را marking the object). خوار کن is another imperative: خوار (low/despised) as resultative adjective + کن (make) — 'make (them) despised/abase them.' Word order: object first, then the imperatival result verb.
segments.s145-g29: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/struck down' (classical past form). تند is an adverb/adjectival modifier 'swift/fiercely'. یک دست 'one hand' is a quantified NP; بر introduces the prepositional phrase 'upon' and links to دست طوس 'Tus's hand' (possessive expressed by juxtaposition). The clause means 'He struck with one swift hand upon Tus's hand.'
segments.s145-g30: تو گفتی functions as reported-speech 'you said' (تو = you; گفتی = you said). ز is the preposition 'from', پیل ژیان is a poetic noun phrase 'elephant [of] life/living' (no modern ezāfe shown), یافت is a past-tense verb 'was found/obtained', and کوس is the object 'war‑drum'. The clause literally: 'You said that from the elephant the drum was found.'
segments.s145-g31: ز بالا = 'from above' (ز = from, بالا = above). نگون is an adverb/adjective meaning 'overturned/downward' modifying the ensuing verbal action. اندرآمد here is an older verbal form meaning 'entered/fell in' (classical usage), and به سر specifies the landing place 'onto the head'. The whole phrase describes someone falling headlong from above onto his head.
segments.s145-g32: برو کرد is a light-verb construction: برو (go/advance) + کرد (made/did) = 'caused to go' or 'set off/moved'. رستم is the subject placed after the verb (VSO order common in classical Persian). به تندی گذر is an adverbial phrase 'with rapid/hasteful passage' (به + تندی forming an adverbial, گذر = passage/passing). Together: 'Rostam set off / passed swiftly.'
segments.s145-g9: Two imperatives joined by و: بگیر (imperative 'take/seize') and ببر (imperative 'carry/take away') share the same (implicit) object. زنده is an adjective 'alive' modifying the action that follows; بر + دار + کن is a light-verb construction where بر (on) + دار (gallows) + کن (imperative of کردن 'do') yields 'put/hang on the gallows'. Word order is typical of Persian commands with modifiers preceding the light verb کن.
segments.s145-g10: وزو نیز is a phrase meaning 'also concerning him' (وزو is a contracted/poetic 'of him/about him' in context; no matching sense was provided). مگردان is a negative imperative formed with م- + گردان meaning 'do not turn/bring/ direct'; with با من it means 'do not address/bring speech to me.' سخن is the object 'speech' or 'talk.'
segments.s145-g11: ز (poetic) introduces the cause/scene 'from/at his words' (ز گفتار او). گیو را دل بخست is literal 'Giv's heart shut' — را marks the person affected (classical usage where را attaches to the affected person) and بخست is a past tense verb 'shut/contracted.'
segments.s145-g12: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'for/that ...'. بردی is a 2nd-person past ('you brought/put/laid'); به رستم is the prepositional complement 'upon/to Rostam'; بر آن‌گونه دست is a noun phrase 'a hand of that kind' (بر = on/upon, آن‌گونه = 'such', دست = 'hand' or 'act'), so the clause says you had placed/used such a hand against Rostam. The verb appears before the small prepositional phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s145-g13: برآشفت is a past tense verb meaning 'became angry' or 'flared up.' It takes prepositional complements with با to indicate at/with whom he was angry: با گیو و با پیلتن 'at Giv and at Piltan.' The verb precedes its complements here, a normal poetic ordering.
segments.s145-g14: فرو ماند is a compound meaning 'remained/was left (down/in a state)'; خیره is a predicative adjective 'stunned/astonished' modifying the (postposed) subject همه انجمن 'the whole assembly'. The verb-first order (predicate then subject) is common in epic poetry: 'deeply remained astonished the entire assembly.'
segments.s145-g15: بفرمود (honorific past) 'he commanded/said' introduces a reported order; پس 'then' places it sequentially. طوس را has را marking the person affected by the command, and شهریار is an appositive/title 'the commander/lord' applied to Tus. The whole phrase means 'then he commanded Tus, the commander.'
segments.s145-g16: که introduces the content of the order (a reported imperative). رو here is an imperative 'go' (poetic/colloquial رو = برو) — no matching noun sense was provided, so it is marked as unmatched. هردو را is the object 'both of them' marked by را; زنده modifies the manner 'alive'; برکن is an imperative/light-verb meaning 'pull up/remove' (here 'hang'); به دار is the direction/place 'onto the gallows.' The sequence gives the command: 'Go — take both alive and hang [them] on the gallows.'
segments.s145-g113: چرا introduces a rhetorical question ('why'); دارم is the present 1st-person 'I have' (I feel/possess). از governs the following noun phrase as a genitive/prepositional marker ('of/from'), so از خشم کاووس = 'of Kavus's anger'. باک is the noun 'fear' that is the object/compliment of دارم.
segments.s145-g114: The correlative چه ... چه ... functions here as 'whether ... or ...' (elliptical 'be' is omitted). کاووس پیشم = 'Kavus before me' ('پیشم' = in front of me/in my presence). یک مشت خاک = 'a handful of dust' (مشت = handful here, خاک = dust), i.e. something utterly insignificant.
segments.s145-g115: سرم گشت سیر is literal 'my head became sated' (گشت = became, past); this is a poetic way to say 'I am satisfied/tired of it'. و links the next clause; دلم کرد بس is idiomatic: دلم ('my heart') + کرد (made/caused/said) + بس ('enough'/'stop'), i.e. 'my heart said enough.'
segments.s145-g116: جز از = 'except (from)'; پاک یزدان = 'the Pure God' (پاک modifying یزدان). نترسم is present negative 1st-person 'I do not fear'; ز کس = 'from anyone' (ز = from, کس = anyone). Together: 'I fear no one except the Pure God.'
segments.s145-g117: ز گفتار = 'from/because of the speech' (ز as a poetic 'from'); چون here functions as 'when/since' introducing a temporal clause; سیر گشت (= became sated) takes انجمن ('assembly/meeting') as the subject: 'when the assembly became sated with talk.'
segments.s145-g118: چنین = 'thus/so' (introducing reported speech). گفت is the past 3rd-person 'he said' (the provided sense list lacks 'said', so mapped to -1). گودرز با پیلتن names the speaker; با here marks association ('Goudarz of/with Piltan').
segments.s145-g119: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/in order that'). شهر و دلیران و لشکر lists the subjects ('the city, the brave ones, and the army'). گمان is the noun 'thought/opinion' (their attention/consideration), preparing the phrase that follows.
segments.s145-g120: به دیگر سخنها = 'to/onto other words/topics' (به = to/towards, دیگر سخنها = other speeches/topics). برند is the present/jussive plural of بردن ('they may carry/turn'), here 'they turn (their) thought toward'; این زمان is the temporal adjunct 'at this time.' Word order places the object phrase before the verb, as typical in Persian.
segments.s145-g145: This is a compact, poetic noun phrase. وزین is a contracted/poetic prepositional link meaning roughly “and/from this” (و + زِ این) that points to what follows. ناسگالیده is an adjective ‘unfavoured/ill‑fated’ modifying بدخواه ‘ill‑wisher/enemy’, and نو post‑modifies بدخواه as ‘new’ (newly hostile). In classical Persian such modifiers commonly follow the noun phrase.
segments.s145-g146: دلم is the subject with the possessive/clitic -م ‘my’ attached (my heart). گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copular/becoming verb ‘became’. باریک is the predicate adjective ‘slender, thin’. چون + simile (چون ماه نو) means ‘like the new moon’. Word order: Subject (دلم) + past verb (گشت) + predicate (باریک) with a simile introduced by چون.
segments.s145-g147: بدین here functions as a prepositional phrase ‘for this / to this’ (به + این). چاره جستن is a verbal-noun/infinitive phrase ‘to seek a remedy’ that serves as the complement/purpose for خواستم. ترا is the object pronoun ‘you’ placed before the verb (archaic/object order), and خواستم is past‑tense 1st person ‘I wished/desired’ (subject implied).
segments.s145-g148: چو دیر آمدی is a temporal subordinate clause ‘when you came late’ (چو = when). تندی آراستم is the main clause: تندی ‘haste/fury’ with آراستم (past 1st person of آراستن) meaning ‘I made/arranged/prepared’ — here idiomatically ‘I made haste’ or ‘I collected/roused my haste’. The verb is past and the subject ‘I’ is the speaker.
segments.s145-g149: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. آزرده is a predicative adjective ‘distressed/hurt’ and گشتی is the 2nd‑person past form ‘you became/grew’ (addressing the addressee). تو‌ای پیلتن is a vocative ‘you, O Piltan’ (تو ای = ‘you O…’). The clause has the usual order: temporal marker + predicate verb + vocative.
segments.s145-g150: پشیمان شدم is a simple past clause ‘I became remorseful/regretful’ (شدم = I became). خاکم اندر دهن is an idiomatic prepositional phrase literally ‘my dust in (my) mouth’ (خاک + possessive -م = my dust), with اندر = in and دهن = mouth; it expresses humiliation/contrition (I humbled myself).
segments.s145-g151: بدو گفت رستم is the reporting clause ‘Rostam said to him’ (بدو = to him/unto him). که introduces reported speech (‘that’/‘that’ clause). گیهان تراست is the content of the speech: گیهان ‘the world’ and تراست = تو را است, an archaic fused form meaning ‘it is yours / belongs to you’. The clause thus states possession: ‘the world is yours.’
segments.s145-g152: همه کهترانیم is a short clause ‘we are all lesser/inferior’ (همه = all; کهترانیم is the plural copula form meaning ‘we are lesser’). و links to the next coordinated clause. فرمان تراست repeats the archaic possessive construction ‘فرمان’ (command/order) + تراست (‘is yours’), i.e. ‘and the command is yours.’ The sentence uses inverted/compact classical constructions and the fused تراست for ‘it is yours.’
segments.s145-g97: ستایش (a noun) is the object/theme 'praise' and precedes the verb; گرفتند is a past tense, 3rd‑person plural of گرفتن used idiomatically here ('to offer/take praise' = 'to praise'). بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase marking the target ('upon the champion' = 'of/for the champion'). The whole clause is a straightforward past narration: 'They praised the champion.'
segments.s145-g98: که introduces a descriptive/subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'). The adjectives جاوید, بادی and روشن‌روان follow without an explicit copula (a common poetic pattern), so they function as permanent/epithet‑like descriptions of the champion; و simply coordinates بادی and روشن‌روان. There is no finite verb inside this short clause—the adjectives stand as attributes or wishes (understood as 'he is' or 'may he be').
segments.s145-g99: The clause is optative: جهان 'the world' is the topic/subject, سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'completely' or 'all over', and زیر پای تو is a locative phrase 'under your foot/feet'. باد here is the optative particle 'may it be' (not 'wind'), placed at the end as is normal in Persian for wishes. Word order is Topic — adverbial — locative — optative verb.
segments.s145-g100: همیشه 'always' is an adverb modifying the wish. سر تخت ('at the head/top of the throne') is a noun phrase where سر = 'top/summit' + تخت 'throne', جای تو 'your place' is the complement, and باد again is the optative 'may it be'. The phrase thus expresses a continual wish that the throne's foremost place belong to you.
segments.s145-g101: تو دانی is a present declarative 'you know'. که introduces the content clause. کاووس را uses the object/topic marker را to mark Kavus as the affected party, and مغز نیست is a predicative negation ('there is no brain' = 'he has no brains/ sense'). The sentence states a known fact about Kavus using present tense نیسـت.
segments.s145-g102: به تیزی سخن is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in/for the sharpness of speech' (به + noun), گفتنش is the nounized infinitive with possessive suffix -ش 'his speaking' ('the way he speaks'), and نغز نیست is the predicate 'is not pithy/witty'. Word order places the adverbial first, then the nominalized verb, then the negative predicate.
segments.s145-g103: بجوشد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form used narratively to mean 'he seethes/boils' (here metaphorical for anger or agitation). همانگه 'at that very moment' modifies the following clause; پشیمان شود is another 3rd‑person singular verb phrase 'becomes regretful' (شود = 'become'). The two verbs are coordinated in sequence: first he seethes, then immediately he repents.
segments.s145-g104: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with goodwill/for the better'. ز سر باز here means 'again, from the head/from the start' (ز + سر = 'from the head' used idiomatically = 'anew'; باز = 'again'). پیمان شود is a passive/intransitive construction 'the covenant/agreement will be made/come about' with شود as the auxiliary; overall: 'the covenant will be renewed from the start, and done well.'
segments.s145-g41: This is a nominal clause listing two instruments as the speaker's companions. Word order is noun + modifier (سر نیزه = "head of the spear"). و simply links تیغ ("sword"). یار is predicate complement ("companion"). من‌اند is an archaic/poetic form related to مانَد/مانند (used here as a copular/predicative particle meaning roughly "are"/"are like"); it links the whole preceding noun phrase to یار.
segments.s145-g42: Another nominal clause parallel to the first. دو بازو ("two arms") and دل شهریار ("the king's heart" with the genitive construction) are coordinated by و. The final من‌اند again functions as the copula/predicative marker (archaic/poetic), tying the coordinated noun phrase to the implicit predicate (they 'are' something for the speaker).
segments.s145-g43: چه introduces an exclamation/rhetorical question ("how/what"). آزاردم is an older/perhaps dialectal/past-stem verb form meaning "cause harm / trouble me" (here rendered rhetorically: "would he harm me?"). او is the third-person pronoun "he." من بنده‌ام is a nominal predicate with the pronoun subject placed before the predicate; بنده‌ام uses bende + -am as a humble self-reference "I am a (humble) servant."
segments.s145-g44: یک‌(ی) here functions as an emphasizing/limiting particle meaning roughly "one/only" or "merely." بنده is again the humble self-reference noun. آفریننده‌ام is a possessed noun (آفریننده + -am) meaning "I am a servant of the Creator;" the whole phrase asserts devotion to the Creator rather than to any mortal.
segments.s145-g45: به ایران is a prepositional phrase "into/to Iran." ار is an archaic conditional particle meaning "if/supposing (that)." ایدون is an archaic/poetic third-person form of آمدن (here optative/subjunctive: "should come"/"comes"). که introduces the subordinate clause identifying the subject: سهراب گرد (سهراب + گرد as an adverbial/qualifying element meaning "about/around/if Sohrab comes about"). Together this segment sets up the conditional "If Sohrab comes to Iran..."
segments.s145-g46: بیاید is the subjunctive/optative third-person form of آمدن used here to continue the conditional ("should come"). نماند is the negative present/past-stem verb meaning "will not remain/there will be none left." بزرگ و نه خرد are the coordinated nouns "the great and (not) the wise"; note نه here negates the second coordinated item and the whole phrase means "neither the great nor the wise (will remain)."
segments.s145-g47: شما هر کسی is a vocative/address to the audience: "you, every one (of you)." چاره جان کنید is an imperative/request with چاره acting as object "a remedy/means" and جان as its complement "for life"; کنید is the present-subjunctive/imperative 2nd-person-plural of کردن used here as a polite command "(do/make) find/prepare." The verb at the end places the action on the addressees.
segments.s145-g48: خرد را is the definite object (را marking 'wisdom' as the thing to be acted on). بدین is a literary compound (به + این) meaning "by this / with this / in this manner." کار پیچان کنید is an imperative phrase: کار پیِچان (پیچان here = 'twisting', hence 'cunning' or 'turning') plus کنید (imperative), so the clause means "employ/turn wisdom to this task" or more naturally "apply your wisdom/cunning to this affair."
segments.s145-g153: کنون آمدم is a past-tense 1st-person statement ('I have come/I've come now'). تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that') and is followed by the present/subjunctive verb دهی (2nd-person singular present/subjunctive 'you give' / 'you may give')—together تا چه فرمان دهی = 'to see/learn what order you will give.' Word order is typical classical Persian: purpose clause after آمدم.
segments.s145-g154: روانت is روان + enclitic -ت ('your soul/spirit') and is the topic/subject of the wish. ز دانش is a prepositional phrase 'from/of knowledge' (ز = 'from/of'). مبادا is a negative optative/precautionary particle 'may it not be / lest', which governs the predicate adjective تهی ('empty / devoid'). There is no finite verb because مبادا + adjective expresses a wish/negative hope.
segments.s145-g155: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' placed before the verb (classical order: indirect object/oblique before گفت). گفت here is the verbal past 'he said' (the supplied sense list lacked a verbal entry, so sense marked -1). کاووس is the subject (placed after the verb for literary inversion). کامروز بزم is a temporal phrase + object: 'today (a) feast' (the object bzm is picked up by the verb in the next segment).
segments.s145-g156: گزینیم and بسازیم are 1st-person plural present/subjunctive or hortative forms ('let us choose' / 'we will choose', 'let us prepare' / 'we will make'); classical Persian often uses this form for proposals. The coordination و links the two verbs; فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow' applying to بسازیم; رزم is the object of بسازیم ('battle'). Word order: object (بزم) was placed earlier for emphasis and matched by بسازیم here.
segments.s145-g157: بیاراست is a past/actionive verbal form meaning 'he adorned / arranged' (classical literary past). It governs the noun phrase رامشگهی شاهوار ('a regal/lofty resting-place') where شاهوار follows and qualifies رامشگهی as an adjective; the whole phrase is the object of بیاراست.
segments.s145-g158: شد is the past/copular verb 'became' (turning point/result). ایوان is the subject ('the iwan/porch'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/likeness of' (به + کردار = 'as / like'), and باغ بهار is a compound noun 'spring garden' (باغ modified by بهار). The clause says the iwan assumed the appearance of a spring garden.
segments.s145-g159: ز introduces the source/origin 'from' (poetic). آواز here functions as 'sound/voice' (the rustling/sound of silk) and is coordinated with بانگ 'the sound/cry' of the نای (ney, reed-flute) by و. The phrase names the musical/sensory sources producing the scene.
segments.s145-g160: سمن is a noun 'jasmine' or 'jasmine-scent' used here as an evocative noun for fragrant/beautiful presences. عارضان is a plural noun or participial/agentive word in apposition (the provided senses do not match the literary sense, so marked -1). پیش + خسرو means 'before Khosrow / in Khosrow's presence', and به پای is a directional phrase 'to (his) feet' (به + پای = toward the foot/at the feet). The whole phrase places the jasmine-scented figures before the king at his feet.
segments.s145-g89: سرش (سر + -ش) is the direct object 'his head' (سر = head, -ش = his). کردن is the light/lexical verb 'to do/make' attached here to form a causative/action phrase; the following از introduces what the head is emptied of: تیزی من = 'my sharpness' (تیزی + من as possessive). تهی is a predicate/adjective meaning 'empty, devoid (of)' and here completes the construction 'از ... تهی کردن' = 'to empty/deprive (of ...)'. Word order is characteristic of older Persian: object (سرش) before the light verb (کردن) and the 'از ... تهی' phrase stating what is removed.
segments.s145-g90: نمودن here is the main verb 'to show/manifest' (used impersonally in narrative), بدو is the dative 'to him' (بـ + او), روزگار is used in the sense 'fate, lot', and بهی is the adjective 'good/excellent'. The clause thus reads literally 'fate showed (to) him good' = 'fate granted him good fortune.'
segments.s145-g91: چو ... برخاست is a temporal clause 'when ... rose'. گودرز is the proper name subject. برخاست is past tense 'rose/rose up/left'. از پیش اوی literally 'from before him' — از (from), پیش here 'in the presence of/before', and اوی is the archaic oblique pronoun 'him'. The clause means 'when Goudarz rose from his presence.'
segments.s145-g92: پس is a connective 'then/thereupon'. پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero'. تیز here functions as an adverbial/attribute 'swift/keen'. بنهاد is an older past form meaning 'placed/set' (here 'set' or 'directed'); followed by روی 'face' meaning 'he turned/set his face (toward)'. The phrase means 'then the hero quickly set his face/turned swiftly.'
segments.s145-g93: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/went off'. با او 'with him' (با = with, او = him). سران سپاه is a genitival noun phrase 'the chiefs/heads of the army' (سران = leaders/heads, سپاه = army). Word order places the verb before its companions as common in narrative past-tense Classical Persian.
segments.s145-g94: پس 'then'. رستم the proper name subject. اندر is an older preposition 'in/into/among' here functioning adverbially with گرفتند راه: گرفتند (past plural of گرفتن) 'took/seized' and راه 'way/road' — the idiom 'گرفتنِ راه' = 'to take the road/set out'. Although گرفتند is plural, it is a common narrative agreement with a previously implied group; the overall sense is 'then Rostam (and company) took the road' or more loosely 'Rostam set out.'
segments.s145-g95: چو دیدند is 'when they saw' (چو = when, دیدند = they saw). گرد here means 'around/area/circle' (an adverbial/noun of locality). گو پیلتن appears as a toponymic phrase (گو + پیلتن) — in this context it's best read as the place-name 'Gu-Piltan' or a local area around that name; the sequence denotes 'the vicinity/area of Gu-Piltan' that they observed. Word order puts the temporal clause first: 'when they saw the area of Gu-Piltan.'
segments.s145-g96: همه 'all/everyone'. نامداران is plural 'the famed/renowned ones' (نامدار = famous person). شدند is past plural 'became/were' (here copular 'became assembled') and انجمن is 'assembly/meeting'. Together they form 'all the renowned men became an assembly' = 'all the famous men assembled.'
segments.s145-g57: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward/near”; it precedes the noun phrase این شاه دیوانه (this mad king), where the adjective دیوانه follows the noun (typical Persian adjective placement). رو here functions as an imperative/command meaning “go/turn (toward)” placed at the end of the clause.
segments.s145-g58: وزین is an adverbial/qualifying word “in weighty/solemn manner” that heads the phrase; در سخن is the prepositional complement “in speech/words.” یاد کن is an imperative construction: the noun یاد (“remembrance/mention”) plus the imperative verb کن (“do”) = “mention/recall.” نو به نو is a reduplicated adverbial phrase meaning “anew” or “again and afresh,” placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s145-g59: سخنهای is the plural noun “speeches/words” followed by the postpositive adjectives چرب و دراز (“flattering and long”) — adjectives come after the noun in Persian. آوری is the finite verb at the end (here describing the addressee’s action: “you bring/produce”), typical SOV ordering.
segments.s145-g60: مگر introduces an exception or conditional sense here: “unless.” The noun phrase بخت گم (“lost fortune/fortune that is lost”) is followed by the auxiliary/participial بوده (“has been/was”) and finally بازآوری, which functions as the verbal idea “(you) restore/bring back” (a nominal/verb form placed at the clause end). The clause thus reads as a conditional: “unless you restore the fortune that has been lost.”
segments.s145-g61: This is a simple nominal subject phrase (title + names: سپهدار گودرز کشواد) followed by the past-tense verb رفت (“went/set out”) placed at the end, following normal classical Persian SOV order. The sequence names the actor before giving the action.
segments.s145-g62: به نزدیک is again a prepositional phrase “to/near,” taking خسرو (the king’s name) as its object; خرامید is the past tense verb “strode/stepped proudly” describing Khosrow’s motion. The final word تفت is an adverbial/particle-like element attached after the verb for a rhetorical or emphatic effect (poetic diction), placed after the verb as is common in epic phrasing.
segments.s145-g63: به کاووس is an indirect object phrase “to Kāvus/for Kāvus.” کی گفت is an interrogative clause “who said/told” (کی = who, گفت = said); the following clause رستم چه کرد is the content clause object meaning “what Rostam did” (رستم = Rostam, چه = what, کرد = did). Word order places the indirect object and question word before the verb, with the reported content immediately after.
segments.s145-g64: کز is a subordinating particle here introducing the consequence/subordinate clause “that/from which”; ایران is the source “from Iran.” برآوردی is the verbal idea placed near the end (here conveying ‘you brought back’ or ‘you caused to be brought back’), امروز is the temporal adverb “today,” and گرد is a final particle with an archaic/modal force that frames the result or hypotheticity — the clause therefore reads as the result/expectation: “that today you brought him back from Iran.”
segments.s145-g81: پشیمان بشد is a past-clause: پشیمان (regretful) is the predicative adjective and بشد (past form of شدن) marks the change of state ('he became/regretted'). زان 'from that / for that reason' + کجا گفته بود is a relative clause ('what/that which he had said') with گفته (past participle) + بود as an auxiliary forming the past perfect. Word order is subject omitted → predicate → complement (the clause explaining the cause).
segments.s145-g82: به بیهودگی مغزش آشفته بود is a simple copular clause: مغزش ('his mind/brain') is the subject, آشفته بود ('was disturbed') is the predicate (آشفته + بود as past copula). به بیهودگی introduces the cause/ground ('because of / by the futility'), with به functioning as a preposition meaning 'by/through' here.
segments.s145-g83: به گودرز گفت is 'he said to Goudarz': به + name marks the indirect object/recipient. گفت here is the reporting verb (past), followed by the clause این سخن درخورست ('this speech is appropriate'). Note: گفته/gفت in the provided senses list lacks the explicit 'said' sense, so the reporting-function is expressed by گفت in context.
segments.s145-g84: لب پیر با پند نیکوترست is a verbless descriptive clause where لب پیر ('the old man's lip/mouth', i.e. his words) is the subject phrase, با پند ('with advice') is an adjunct, and نیکوترست ('is better') is the predicate (درست/تر-form + copula). The order is noun phrase + adjunct + predicate, typical in poetry.
segments.s145-g85: خردمند باید دل پادشا: خردمند is the (implied) subject 'the wise person'; باید is a modal 'must/ought to'. دل پادشا ('the king's heart') is the object/goal. The verb 'to gain/win' is omitted (elliptical) — classical/poetic Persian often omits an expected infinitive, so باید governs the understood action of securing the king's heart.
segments.s145-g86: که introduces the reason/clause 'that/for' followed by the coordinated noun phrase تیزی و تندی ('sharpness and fierceness/harshness') and the verb phrase نیارد بها expressing negative result. Here نیارد is a negative/archaic form meaning 'does not bring' (not listed in the provided senses), and بها ('value/price') is the object: 'that sharpness and harshness bring no value.'
segments.s145-g87: شما را بباید بر او شدن: شما را (topic/object-marked 'you') + بباید (modal 'must/ought') + بر او شدن is a verbal phrase meaning 'to go to/approach him' (بر + pronoun + شدن). Persian here places the object/topic before the modal; شدن is the infinitive 'to become/go' used idiomatically with بر to mean 'approach.'
segments.s145-g88: به خوبی بسی داستانها زدن: به خوبی ('with goodness / courteously') is an adverbial phrase (به + noun used adverbially), بسی here functions adverbially/quantifying ('much/many') modifying داستانها ('stories'), and زدن is the verbal infinitive. In classical Persian داستان زدن means 'to tell/narrate tales' (the simple dictionary senses for زدن given do not list 'to narrate').
segments.s145-g65: فراموش کردی is a past tense second-person form meaning “you forgot / you have forgotten” with the subject (تو) omitted. The prepositional phrase ز هاماوران follows the verb and specifies the object/topic (‘about/from Hamavaran’) using poetic ز for ‘about/of’. The overall unit reads literally “you forgot (of) Hamavaran.”
segments.s145-g66: وزان is a compact conjunctional phrase (‘and of that / and from that’), linking this phrase to what was just mentioned. کار here means an affair/deed (the sense ‘deed/affair’), and دیوان مازندران refers to the devs/diwan associated with Mazandaran (the lexical senses given do not include ‘demon’ here, so the entry is left as unmatched). Together it gives ‘and that deed/affair of the divs of Mazandaran.’
segments.s145-g67: که introduces reported speech/explanation (‘that’ / ‘namely’). گویی here functions as ‘you say’ (introducing quoted or paraphrased words), followed by ورا ‘him/it’, زنده ‘alive’, بر دار ‘upon the gallows’ and the imperative کن ‘do!’ — together: “you say: hang him alive on the gallows.” Note the imperative کن attaches to the verb phrase بر دار.
segments.s145-g68: ز شاهان is a prepositional phrase indicating relation to the kings (classical poetic use); نباید is impersonal ‘one must not/it is not proper to’, followed by گزافه سخن ‘exaggerated/nonsensical speech’. The clause means ‘one should not speak extravagant or empty words to (or concerning) kings.’
segments.s145-g69: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’. او رفت و آمد is a compound expression ‘he went and came / his comings and goings / his movements’ describing arrival/activity; سپاهی بزرگ (lit. ‘a great سپاهی’) follows as the noun phrase ‘a great army/host’ appearing in the scene. Word order places the temporal clause first, then the appearance of the host.
segments.s145-g70: یکی here means ‘a certain / one (of them)’. پهلوانی is used as a noun denoting a champion/hero (the lexical choices offered are ‘heroism/wrestling’ but context requires a heroic person). به کردار = ‘in the manner/behavior of’, and گرگ ‘wolf’ is a simile describing his bearing. The phrase describes ‘a certain champion who behaved like a wolf.’
segments.s145-g71: This relative/conditional clause uses که داری ‘that you have / do you have’ to ask whether the addressee possesses anyone such that با او به دشت نبرد ‘a battle will be (joined) with him on the plain’. داری is present/past-stem ‘have’, and نبرد is the noun ‘battle’ (here functioning as the action expected to take place).
segments.s145-g72: شود is the auxiliary/optative sense ‘may it be / it will happen’, taking برفشاند (subjunctive/infinitival sense ‘to scatter / to hurl’) as the main action; برو is an imperative ‘go’ or hortative ‘let him go’, and تیره گرد is a compact phrase ‘become dark / turn gloomy’ (تیره = dark, گرد here a verbalizing element ‘become/turn’). Together the verbs form a sequence of desire/command: ‘let him go; he will scatter (the foe) and turn things dark/gloomy.’
segments.s145-g137: بدو گفت = "he said to him"; the prepositional element بدو (to him) comes before the verb گفت (said) — a common poetic order. اگر introduces a conditional clause; بیم دارد دلم literally orders verb before the possessed noun (دارد = has, دل + enclitic -م = "my heart"): "if my heart has fear / if my heart is afraid."
segments.s145-g138: نخواهم is first-person negative (ن + خواهم) meaning "I do not want / I would not wish." که introduces the subordinate clause, with باشد as a present subjunctive/optative meaning roughly "that it be / that it should be." ز = "from" (poetic) + تن = "the body," and بگسلم (from گسل) is a verbal idea "become separated / be torn away" — here expressing 'to be separated from the body' (i.e. to die or be parted).
segments.s145-g139: ازین ننگ = "from this shame/disgrace" (از + این in poetic spelling). برگشت و آمد are two past actions joined by و: "turned back and came." به راه = "onto/along the road." The verb cluster برگشت و آمد is simple past narrative.
segments.s145-g140: گرازان و پویان are coordinated nouns following the verb phrase and function as those moving along the road; گرازان = "boars" (or a band of boars) and پویان = "the moving/active ones" (i.e. the swift runners). به نزدیک = prepositional phrase 'toward/near,' شاه is the goal/locational noun "the king."
segments.s145-g141: چو = temporal "when." در شد is an archaic perfective/preterite construction meaning "entered / went in." ز در = "from/through the door/gate," and شاه is the subject of the following clause: شاه بر پای خاست = "the king rose to his feet" (بر پای خاست is a fixed phrase meaning 'stood up'). Word order places the entrance clause before the king's reaction.
segments.s145-g142: بخواست is past tense 'he requested/asked.' بسی پوزش = 'many/much apology(s)' (بسی intensifies), اندر گذشته = 'regarding the past' or 'for what had happened.' The whole clause means 'he asked for many apologies for what had passed.'
segments.s145-g143: که introduces a clause 'that...' تندی = 'swiftness' or 'haste'; مرا (here written as م + را in the segment) is the older/genitive form meaning 'of me / my.' گوهرست is a nominal predicate 'is a jewel/precious thing' and سرشت = 'nature, disposition' is coordinated by و: 'is my jewel and (is) my nature.'
segments.s145-g144: چنان زیست باید = 'one must live thus' or 'such a life is required' (زیست here = to live / manner of living, باید = modal 'must/ought'). که introduces the result/standard clause; یزدان = 'God' or 'the divine,' and بکشت is an archaic verbal form (optative/subjunctive flavor) meaning essentially 'would kill/slay' — the sense is 'so live that (only) God might be the slayer' (i.e. live so bravely that only a divine force could take you).
segments.s145-g49: This clause reads as a negative injunction with time: 'به ایران' is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb (literal 'to/in Iran'); 'نبینید' is the 2nd-person plural negative present/jussive ('do not see/let [me] see'). 'ازین پس' = 'from now on' is composed of از + این (here the form ین) + پس. The final 'مرا' is the object 'me' (written here as م + را), so the whole string means 'do not let me see Iran henceforth.'
segments.s145-g50: Two contrasted topical phrases: 'شما را زمین' uses را as a topic/emphasis marker after 'you'—'as for you, the land (is yours)'. The next phrase 'پر کرگس مرا' contrasts the speaker's view: 'پر' = 'full (of)' + noun 'کرگس' (vultures), and the trailing 'مرا' (m + را) marks the speaker's perspective ('for me'). The clause therefore contrasts 'the land is yours' with 'for me it is full of vultures.'
segments.s145-g51: 'غمی شد' uses شد ('befell/happened', here past) with the noun غمی ('a grief') to mean 'a grief came upon'. 'دل نامداران' is a genitive construction 'the heart(s) of the famed/renowned'; 'همه' follows to quantify 'all' (i.e. 'the hearts of all the distinguished').
segments.s145-g52: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'); 'رستم شبان بود' is a simple past copular clause 'Rostam was a shepherd.' The coordinate 'و ایشان رمه' is elliptical: the verb 'بود' is omitted but understood, giving 'and they (were) a flock.'
segments.s145-g53: 'به گودرز گفتند' = 'they said to Goudarz' (prepositional indirect object before the verb). 'کاین کار تست' is a reported clause introduced by کاین ('that/this'); the final word here is an archaic copular/pronominal form meaning 'it is yours' (attested in classical Persian). The lemma/senses supplied in the word-list do not reflect this archaic copula, so that sense is indicated below as unavailable.
segments.s145-g54: 'شکسته' is a past participle/adjective 'broken'; 'به دست تو' is the agent/means phrase 'by your hand' (literally 'to/at the hand of you'); 'گردد' is a subjunctive/jussive form of گردیدن used here to express a wished/result clause 'may it be made/occur'; 'درست' is the predicate adjective 'right/correct'. Together: 'may the broken thing be made right by your hand.'
segments.s145-g55: 'سپهبد' is the subject. 'جز از تو' = 'except from you' (جز + از + pronoun); 'سخن نشنود' places the object 'speak/speech' before the verb: 'he will not hear speech except from you' (نشنود is a negative-present/optative form meaning 'does not/will not hear' in classical usage).
segments.s145-g56: 'همی' is an adverb indicating continuity/emphasis ('still/indeed'); 'بخت تو' is 'your fortune' (note Persian often places the possessor after the noun). 'زین سخن' is an archaic form meaning 'from this speech' (زین = از این), but the supplied senses do not include this use; 'نغنود' is the negative present/optative of غنود 'to sleep/slumber', here figuratively 'be pacified/soothed' — 'your fortune will not be calmed by these words.'
segments.s145-g129: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing source; سهراب یل is a noun phrase with apposition ('Sohrab, the hero'). رفت is used idiomatically with سخن (literally 'speech went') to mean 'began to speak' or 'uttered'; یکسر modifies the verb phrase as 'at once/straightaway' and سخن is the thing uttered ('a speech'). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: 'from Sohrab the hero there went forth at once a speech.'
segments.s145-g130: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. پشت بر + NP is an idiomatic direction phrase 'one's back toward X' (here 'پشت بر شاه ایران' = 'one's back toward the king of Iran'). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (classical jussive form). The literal order 'thus back toward the king do not (make)' yields the natural command 'do not thus turn your back on the king of Iran.'
segments.s145-g131: چنین 'thus' sets the resultative tone. بر شده is a compound meaning 'has become / has been established' (شده = become/been) and نامت is the possessed noun 'your name / your fame'; اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world.' The phrase means 'your name has thus become known in the world.'
segments.s145-g132: بدین is functioning as 'by/with/for this (reason)' (here written as two pieces in the segmentation); بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return' and مگردان is the negative imperative 'do not turn/do not return.' نهان modifies the verb as 'into hiding' or 'secretly.' Together: 'do not, because of this, return to hiding.'
segments.s145-g133: و دیگر که introduces another point: و دیگر 'and moreover/another thing', که is the subordinating particle 'that/when', and تنگ اندرآمد سپاه literally describes the army/host closing in ('تنگ' = close/tight, اندرآمد = came in/entered, سپاه = host/army). The clause means 'and furthermore, when the army has come in tightly...' (i.e., when the host presses in).
segments.s145-g134: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not.' تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous/bleak' used here as 'cast gloom.' بر + خیره marks the target/dative ('upon/over the astonished' — خیره = those staring/astonished). این تاج و گاه is the direct thing affected ('this crown and seat/time'); word order is poetic, so the command reads 'do not cast gloom upon the astonished concerning this crown and seat.'
segments.s145-g135: به رستم marks the indirect object 'to/for Rostam.' بر این داستانها here marks the content/topic 'on these tales' (practically 'these tales'), and بخواند is the narrative past 'he recited/then he read/recited.' The clause means 'he recited these stories to Rostam.'
segments.s145-g136: تهمتن is an epithet/name (Tahmātan, here referring to Rostam). چو is the conjunction 'when.' بشنید is the past tense 'he heard.' خیره بماند is a periphrastic result: خیره 'staring/astonished' + بماند 'he remained/was left' — together 'he remained astonished' or 'he stood gazing in amazement.'
segments.s145-g73: This phrase is an argument phrase whose verb appears in the following segment. یلان (subject) = 'the tribes/people'. ترا is actually ت (2nd-person suffix) + را (object marker), so ترا means 'you' as the definite object. سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial sequence ('سر' + به + 'سر') meaning 'through and through / from beginning to end' and modifies the upcoming verbs. گژدهم is a proper name (appositive to ترا) identifying the 'you' being spoken about.
segments.s145-g74: شنیده‌ست و دیده‌ست are perfect (reported) forms meaning 'has heard' and 'has seen' (3rd singular with the enclitic -st). The conjunction و links the two verbs. از introduces the range 'from ...', and بیش و کم is a fixed pair meaning 'more and less' (i.e. 'in large and small matters' or 'in all respects'). Altogether: 'have heard and seen ... from more and less.'
segments.s145-g75: همی is an adverb signalling habitual or continuous action ('keeps, continually'). گوید is the present (or historic-present) form 'he says / he kept saying' and here introduces what follows; آن روز is a temporal adverbial 'that day'. هرگز مباد is a conventional negative optative: هرگز 'never' + مباد 'may it not be' = 'may it never happen'.
segments.s145-g76: که introduces the content clause (that / which). با او = 'with him'. سواری کند is the verbal phrase 'to ride / to fight mounted (with suvari کردن); کند is the (present/subjunctive) verbal form here governed by the optative in the previous segment ('may it not be that ...'). رزم یاد is a noun phrase 'battle + remembrance' used idiomatically for engaging in or recalling battle — here best read as 'fight / go to battle'.
segments.s145-g77: کسی را is a (definite/particularized) indefinite 'someone' with the object/topic marker را; this whole noun phrase is taken into a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that'. جنگی is an adjective/noun 'warlike / a warrior', چو 'like', رستم the proper name, and بود is the past copula 'was'. So: 'someone who in war was like Rostam.'
segments.s145-g78: بیازارد is a (subjunctive/optative or habitual) verb meaning 'would harm / should annoy'. او را is the object 'him' (او + را). خرد کم بود is a small clause: خرد 'wisdom' (subject) + کم بود 'was little' (past), meaning 'there was little wisdom' — idiomatically 'one would be little wise / it would be foolish' to do the preceding action. Syntactically: 'He who would trouble him had little wisdom.'
segments.s145-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction here = 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. گفتار is the object 'the speech/words'. گودرز شاه places the proper name before the title (apposition): 'Goudarz, the king' (the noun order is name + title).
segments.s145-g80: بدانست is past 'he knew / he perceived'. کاو here is an archaic fused form equivalent to modern که او ('that he'), introducing the content of his knowledge; the provided lemma senses do not match this usage. دارد is a present-tense verb 'has/possesses' used inside the reported clause (sequence-of-tense allowing present after past reporting). آیین و راه are coordinated nouns 'custom / practice and way / path' — the qualities he perceived the man to have.
segments.s145-g105: تهمتن‌گر is a titular/epithetic subject ('the heroic one'). آزرده گردد uses the classical verb گردیدن in a subjunctive/hypothetical sense (literally 'becomes/offended'); ز شاه is an ablative/agential phrase 'by/from the king' (poetic ز marking cause/agent). Word order is subject + predicate + ablative, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s145-g106: هم introduces the following phrase as 'also/too' or 'even'. ایرانیان را uses را to topicalize/emphasize 'the Iranians' (classical Persian uses را not only as direct-object marker but to mark the affected topic). نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative of بودن here functioning as 'there will not be / it is not'; گناه is the predicate noun 'fault/blame' following the verb, i.e. 'there will be no blame (on the Iranians)'.
segments.s145-g107: هم او places emphasis on the person ('he also / he, too'). زان سخنها is an ablative 'from/for those words' (زان = 'from that / those'); پشیمان شده‌ست is a perfect/perfect-like construction: پشیمان (regretful) + شده‌ست ('has become / has been'), i.e. 'has become regretful' or 'has regretted'.
segments.s145-g108: ز تندی is a causal/ablative phrase 'from / because of haste'. بخاید is an older/poetic verb form used here with habitual/iterative همی (here 'همی' = 'continually/still'), so the construction implies a repeated physical gesture; پشت دست ('back of the hand') is the noun phrase that follows. The exact nuance of بخاید is archaic/poetic in Ferdowsi and here indicates a recurrent action toward the back of the hand (a gesture of vexation or regret).
segments.s145-g109: تهمتن is the speaker's name/epithet (here nominative subject). چنین پاسـخ آورد places the adverb چنین ('thus/so') before پاسـخ آورد ('brought/uttered an answer'); آورد is a past-tense narrative verb 'brought/uttered' and باز after it is an adverb meaning 'again' or 'in reply'.
segments.s145-g110: که introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that / namely'). هستم is first-person present 'I am'. ز کاووس can be read as 'of/from Kavus' (a genitive/affiliative use: 'I belong to Kavus' or 'I am from Kavus'). کی is a rhetorical/exclamatory particle here ('who' / 'who would be'); بی‌نیاز is the predicate adjective 'free of need / self-sufficient'. The clause is a brief rhetorical rejoinder: 'I am of Kavus — who is without need?'
segments.s145-g111: مرا here functions as a possessive/topic marker ('for me' / 'my'): in classical Persian word order the possessor can be fronted with مرا. تخت زین باشد is a copular clause with باشد as the copula/subjunctive 'is'—literally 'for me, the saddle is a throne'. و links the second parallel predication تاج ترگ 'a rag is (my) crown'. The construction inverts modern Persian possessive order but is normal in epic/classical diction.
segments.s145-g112: قبا جوشن lists garments/armour ('robe and cuirass') as possessions or attire; و connects that list to دل نهاده به مرگ, where دل (heart) is the direct object and نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed/put' (i.e. 'has set'). به مرگ is the prepositional phrase 'toward/to death'. The whole clause means '(I have) robe and armor, and have set my heart upon death' — a compact epic way to say he is ready to die.
segments.s145-g33: This is a string of short prepositional phrases with two past verbs. 'به در شد' literally 'became/to the door' — here functioning as a terse poetic motion: 'he went/turned to the gate' (شد is the past of شدن). 'به خشم اندرآمد' uses اندرآمد (here a verb) with the preposition به + خشم: literally 'anger entered into him' = 'he became angry'. Finally 'به رخش' is a prepositional phrase 'toward Rakhsh' (the horse); the three parts are linked as quick snapshots of action and direction typical of Ferdowsi's compressed style.
segments.s145-g34: Word order is inverted: 'منم گفت شیراوژن و تاج‌بخش' reports speech. 'منم' functions as the self-identifying phrase 'It is I / I am'. 'گفت' is the past-reporting verb 'said' (here the lexical list did not include the quoted-say sense). The following two items are names/epithets—'شیراوژن و تاج‌بخش'—the words uttered or the titles given; the conjunction و simply links them.
segments.s145-g35: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. 'خشم آورم' is a first‑person verb phrase: آورم (present/subjunctive form of آوردن) with خشم — idiomatically 'I bring/feel anger' = 'I grow angry'. The remainder 'شاه کاووس کیست' is a rhetorical question: 'who is Shah Kavus?' with کی (who) + ست (copula 'is').
segments.s145-g36: This is a second rhetorical question. چرا 'why' asks for motive; 'دست یازد به من' is an idiom meaning 'lay a hand on me / attack me' (دست = hand; یازد is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning to reach/strike at). The clause ends with another question 'طوس کیست' = 'who is Tus?' (ki + st).
segments.s145-g37: Two parallel possessive/copular clauses: 'زمین بنده ... من‌ست' and 'رخش گاه ... من‌ست' (the copula من‌ست = 'belongs to me / is mine'). 'زمین بنده' = 'the earth (is) a servant' (bende = servant). 'رخش گاه' uses گاه in a locative/positional sense here (archaic/poetic: 'place/seat'), so together: 'the earth is my servant and Rakhsh my seat/mount.'
segments.s145-g38: Another paired genitive/possessive line: 'نگین گرز' = 'the jeweled (head) of the mace' or 'jeweled mace', 'مغفر کلاه' = 'the helmet's covering/veil' — the two nouns are linked by و and finished with 'من‌ست' = 'belong to me'. The construction lists the speaker's martial ornaments as personal property.
segments.s145-g39: 'شب تیره' = 'a dark night' (direct description). 'از تیغ رخشان کنم' uses از in an instrumental/agentive sense 'by/with the gleaming sword' and the verb کنم (first person present/subjunctive/intentional) 'I will make/do' — together: 'I will make a dark night by my shining sword' (i.e., I will blot out the day or bring doom with my blade).
segments.s145-g40: 'به آورد گه' is a fixed poetic timing phrase 'when the time/occasion to bring (it) comes' (به + آورد + گه = 'at the bringing-occasion' = 'when the time comes'). 'بر سر افشان کنم' literally 'I will scatter upon the head' (بر سر = upon the head/over them; افشان = scattering; کنم = I will do) — i.e. 'I will bring it and scatter it upon their heads.'
segments.s145-g121: کزین is the classical compound of کز + این meaning “because of this / from this.” ترک is the noun “Turk” followed by the participial adjective ترسنده (here meaning “frightening/terrifying”) as an attributive; there is no modern ezāfe because of the poem’s compressed word order. شد + سرفراز is the past copula verb (شد = “became”) followed by the predicate adjective سرفراز (“proud, victorious”).
segments.s145-g122: همی + رفت is a classical/perfective sequence used iteratively — همی gives an ongoing or habitual sense so همی رفت = “kept going / proceeded.” زین گونه is a compressed demonstrative phrase meaning “in this manner” (زین = this + گونه = manner), here without an explicit ezāfe because of poetic style. چندی به راز is an adverbial string: چندی = “for a while,” and به راز = “in secret / secretly.”
segments.s145-g123: که چونان که begins a subordinate clause meaning “for/as/just as.” گژدهم is a proper name (the messenger or informant); داد is the past verb “gave,” and آگهی is its object “news/notice/information.” The clause therefore states the time/cause: “when Gozdeham gave the news.”
segments.s145-g124: همه بوم و بر is a frozen noun phrase meaning “all the homeland and dwellings / every town and countryside.” کرد is the past verb “made” or “caused,” and باید + تهی expresses necessity of the result: باید تهی = “must be emptied / ought to be void.” So the phrase says the lands and habitations had to be emptied. Note the poetic pairing بوم و بر (land and dwelling) rather than a literal grammatical breakdown of بر.
segments.s145-g125: چو here is the conditional/temporal “when / if.” رستم is the subject name. همی + بترسد is a present/habitual construction (همی gives iterative sense; بترسد is the present/subjunctive-form of ترسیدن with the prefixed ب- used classically to indicate present/habitual), so همی بترسد = “(he) fears / is afraid.” زو is the contracted poetic form of از + او = “from/of him,” here marking the object of fear; به جنگ is a locative/goal phrase “in battle / on the battlefield.”
segments.s145-g126: مرا و ترا (here split in the manuscript as م + را and ت + را) = “me and you” with the object/topic marker را attached. نیست is the copula negative “there is not / is not available.” جای درنگ = “a place/opportunity for delay.” Together: “For you and me there is no place to tarry / we cannot afford to delay.” Note classical ordering and the poetic splitting of pronouns and their را markers.
segments.s145-g127: از here marks cause or source (“because of / on account of”). آشفتن is used as a noun-like verbal noun meaning “confusion/disorder/tumult” (the phrase reads as “the king’s tumult”). شاه is the possessor, and پیگار اوی is a coordinated phrase “and his fighting/struggle,” where اوی is the possessive pronoun “his.” The whole phrase gives the cause/background: “because of the king’s disorder and his fighting.”
segments.s145-g128: بدیدم is first-person past “I saw.” به درگاه is a locative phrase “at the royal court / in the royal presence.” بر + گفت‌وگوی here forms an adverbial/participial sense “engaged in conversation” or “in talk” (classical Persian uses بر with verbal nouns to indicate being in the act of something). So: “I saw (them) at the court conversing.”

sentences.s145-s5: Seize and carry them; hang them alive — and do not address me about him either.

sentences.s145-s7: He flew into a rage at Giv and at Piltan; the whole assembly stood there astonished.
sentences.s145-s8: Then he ordered Tus, the commander: 'Go and hang both of them alive on the gallows.'
sentences.s145-s57: Why should I fear Kavus's wrath — whether Kavus stands before me or is but a handful of dust?
sentences.s145-s58: My head has become sated and my heart has said 'enough'; except for the Pure God, I fear no one.
sentences.s145-s59: When the assembly grew weary of speech, Goudarz of Piltan said thus.
sentences.s145-s60: So that the city, the brave men and the army might turn their thoughts to other words at this time.
sentences.s145-s73: And from this ill‑favored, newly malevolent enemy my heart became slender like the new moon.
sentences.s145-s74: For this remedy I wished to seek you; when you came late I made haste.
sentences.s145-s75: When you were distressed, O Piltan, I became remorseful, dust in my mouth.
sentences.s145-s76: Rostam said to him: ‘The world belongs to you; we are all lesser, and the command is yours.’
sentences.s145-s49: They praised the champion, calling him eternal, wind‑borne, and bright‑souled.
sentences.s145-s50: May the whole world be beneath your feet; may the place at the head of the throne always be yours.
sentences.s145-s51: You know that Kavus has no brain; his speech lacks sharpness and is not witty.
sentences.s145-s52: He will boil (with anger) and immediately become remorseful; then, with goodwill, the covenant will be made anew.
sentences.s145-s21: The spear's head and the sword are my comrades; the two arms and the heart are my sovereign.
sentences.s145-s22: How could he harm me? No — I am not his servant; I am merely a servant of the Creator.
sentences.s145-s23: If Sohrab should come into Iran, neither the great nor the wise will remain.
sentences.s145-s24: Each of you find a way to save your life; apply your wisdom to this matter.
sentences.s145-s77: I have come now to learn what command you will give; may your soul never be empty of knowledge.
sentences.s145-s78: Kavus said to him, 'Today we will choose a feast, and tomorrow we will prepare for battle.'
sentences.s145-s79: He adorned the royal resting-place; the iwan became like a spring garden.
sentences.s145-s80: From the rustle/sound of silk and the tone of the ney, jasmine‑scented (figures) came before Khosrow at his feet.
sentences.s145-s45: They made his head empty of my sharpness; fate showed him a good lot.
sentences.s145-s46: When Goudarz rose from before him, then the champion quickly turned his face.
sentences.s145-s47: The chiefs of the army went off with him; then Rostam took the road.
sentences.s145-s48: When they saw the circle/area of Gu-Piltan, all the renowned men became assembled.
sentences.s145-s29: Go up to this mad king, and in grave words recount it afresh, anew.
sentences.s145-s30: You bring forth flattering and long speeches — unless you can restore the fortune that was lost.
sentences.s145-s31: Commander Goudarz Kushvad set out; he came up to Khosrow, who strode proudly.
sentences.s145-s32: Who told Kāvus what Rostam had done, so that today you would bring him back from Iran?
sentences.s145-s41: He became regretful for what he had said; his mind was disturbed by its futility.
sentences.s145-s42: He said to Goudarz, 'This remark is fitting; an old man's words with counsel are preferable.'
sentences.s145-s43: A wise man should win the king's heart, for sharpness and harshness bring no worth.
sentences.s145-s44: You should go to him; courteously tell many stories.
sentences.s145-s33: Have you forgotten Hamavaran and that affair/deed of the divs of Mazandaran?
sentences.s145-s34: That is, you say, “Hang him alive on the gallows!” — one must not speak extravagant words to kings.

sentences.s145-s36: If you have anyone who will fight him on the plain, let him go — he will scatter (the foe) and make the scene dark.

sentences.s145-s71: When he entered the gate, the king rose to his feet; he demanded many apologies for what had passed.
sentences.s145-s72: That swiftness is my jewel and my nature; one must live so that even the gods might slay (one).

sentences.s145-s27: They told Goudarz that this matter is yours; may the broken thing be set right by your hand.
sentences.s145-s28: The spahbed will hear no speech except from you; your fortune will not be soothed by these words.
sentences.s145-s65: Sohrab the hero at once began to speak: "Do not thus turn your back on the king of Iran."
sentences.s145-s66: Thus has your name been made in the world; do not, on that account, go back into hiding.

sentences.s145-s39: He who in war was like Rostam—one who would trouble him had little wisdom.
sentences.s145-s40: When King Goudarz heard the speech, he perceived that the man possessed custom and the proper way.
sentences.s145-s53: If the heroic one is offended by the king, it will not be the Iranians' fault either.
sentences.s145-s54: He, too, has regretted those words; from haste he keeps (gesturing to) the back of his hand.

sentences.s145-s56: For me the saddle is a throne and a rag a crown; (I have) a robe, armor, and a heart set on death.
sentences.s145-s17: He went to the gate; anger entered him toward Rakhsh. "It is I," said Shirāvajen and Taj-bakhsh.
sentences.s145-s18: If I become angry, who is King Kavus? Why should Tus lay a hand on me — who is he?
sentences.s145-s19: The earth is my servant and Rakhsh my mount; the jeweled mace and the helmet's covering are my headgear.
sentences.s145-s20: I will make the night dark with my gleaming sword; when the occasion comes I will bring it and scatter it upon their heads.

sentences.s145-s62: For when Gozdeham gave the news, all the towns and countryside had to be emptied.
sentences.s145-s63: If (or when) Rostam fears him in battle, you and I have no place to delay.
sentences.s145-s64: Because of the king’s disorder and his fighting, I saw them at the royal court engaged in conversation.
